ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for grilled chicken cartilage skewer; mapped to the nearest parent class 'chicken food product' (FOODON_03315116) as an approximate anchor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated code for chicken cartilage skewer; A00LC (poultry meat, cooked) used as the closest defensible anchor. Cartilage is a connective tissue product distinct from muscle meat.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT clinical food concept found for grilled chicken cartilage skewer as of 2026.

serving.natural_language_portion: One skewer of grilled chicken cartilage weighing approximately 30 g, a typical single-serve street-food portion.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Chicken, broilers or fryers, separable connective tissue, cooked (SR Legacy ID 05358); adapted with marinade sodium contribution estimated from standard soy-sauce-based grilled preparation
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA SR Legacy does not list a dedicated 'grilled chicken cartilage skewer' entry. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central data for cooked chicken connective tissue (SR Legacy), with sodium adjusted upward to 600 mg/100 g to reflect soy-sauce-based marinade typical of Chinese-style grilled cartilage skewers. Input record values (protein 21.67 g, fat 16.67 g, carbohydrate 3.33 g, energy 250 kcal per 100 g) are broadly consistent with USDA cooked chicken connective tissue data and are retained as the best available estimate. Calcium (16.67 mg/100 g) and iron (1.0 mg/100 g) are also consistent with USDA reference data for cooked chicken tissue.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index not applicable — food contains negligible carbohydrates; GL estimated from available carbohydrate per serving
gi_source.source_match_notes: Grilled chicken cartilage contains only 3.33 g carbohydrate per 100 g, of which the majority is from marinade sugars. At a 30 g serving, available carbohydrate is approximately 1.0 g, yielding a glycemic load near zero. GI is not meaningfully applicable for this food as the carbohydrate content is negligible and insufficient to produce a measurable glycemic response. glycemic_index is set to 0 as a numeric sentinel indicating not-applicable status for a negligible-carbohydrate food; gi_category is 'not_applicable'. Glycemic load per serving is reported as 0.0.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium at 600 mg/100 g — at the upper threshold for high-sodium classification — driven by soy sauce and Shaoxing wine marinade
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit consumption to one skewer (30 g, ~180 mg sodium) and request low-sodium soy sauce substitution; avoid adding extra condiments or salt.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate (3.33 g/100 g) and sugar (1.67 g/100 g) present minimal glycaemic load, but high fat content (16.67 g/100 g) may impair insulin sensitivity with regular consumption
type2_diabetes.guidance: One skewer (30 g) contributes approximately 1 g of carbohydrate and poses negligible acute glycaemic risk, but individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should monitor overall fat intake and limit portion to one to two skewers per sitting.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat at 5.0 g/100 g and cholesterol at 83.33 mg/100 g, combined with high sodium (600 mg/100 g), present compounded cardiovascular risk factors
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit to one skewer occasionally, request reduced-sodium marinade, and account for the saturated fat and cholesterol contribution within their daily cardiovascular dietary targets.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Grilled Chicken Cartilage Skewer (掌中宝, Zhang Zhong Bao) is a popular Chinese street-food snack consisting of chicken knee cartilage threaded onto a skewer and grilled over charcoal or an open flame. The cartilage is typically marinated in a mixture of soy sauce, Shaoxing rice wine, ginger, garlic, cumin, chilli powder, and sesame oil before grilling, which imparts a savoury, slightly smoky, and mildly spiced flavour. The defining characteristic of this dish is its unique chewy, crunchy texture — the cartilage does not soften fully during cooking, producing a satisfying bite that distinguishes it from conventional grilled chicken meat. Per 100 g, the dish provides approximately 250 kcal, 21.67 g protein, 16.67 g fat (of which 5.0 g is saturated), and only 3.33 g carbohydrate, making it a high-protein, low-carbohydrate snack. Sodium is elevated at 600 mg/100 g due to the soy-sauce-based marinade, which is a key clinical consideration for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. A single skewer weighs approximately 30 g and delivers around 75 kcal. The dish is widely sold at Chinese barbecue stalls and night markets across Singapore and Southeast Asia, and is enjoyed as a snack or side dish at any time of day. It is not suitable for vegetarians, vegans, or those avoiding soy or alcohol-based ingredients.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a chicken cartilage skewer?
Is chicken cartilage skewer high in sodium?
Can diabetics eat chicken cartilage skewer?
Is chicken cartilage skewer suitable for hypertension?
What does chicken cartilage taste like?
How is Zhang Zhong Bao prepared?

confidence_reason: No dedicated approved-source entry exists for grilled chicken cartilage skewer. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central data for cooked chicken connective tissue with sodium adjusted to reflect soy-sauce marinade. Input values are broadly consistent with the USDA reference and are retained as the best available estimate. Confidence is medium rather than high because the adaptation involves marinade sodium estimation and the USDA source does not exactly match the prepared dish.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile for grilled chicken cartilage with soy-sauce marinade is not available in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB database as of 2026. potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: micronutrient and moisture data for this specific grilled cartilage preparation are not reported in available approved sources (USDA FoodData Central, Singapore HPB database). trans_fat_g is 0.0: confirmed negligible for grilled chicken cartilage per USDA reference data. vitamin_c_mg is 0.0: confirmed negligible for a meat-based product with no vitamin-C-contributing ingredients in meaningful quantity. Glycemic index is set to numeric 0 as a sentinel value indicating not-applicable status; gi_category is 'not_applicable' because this food contains negligible carbohydrates (3.33 g/100 g); at a 30 g serving, available carbohydrate is approximately 1.0 g and glycemic load is effectively zero. Halal status is marked false because the marinade contains Shaoxing wine (alcohol); the dish may be prepared halal-certified if alcohol-free marinade is used. The input source URL (https://foodo.sg/) was not used as a nutrient evidence source as it is not an approved primary nutrient database; USDA FoodData Central was used instead.

q1_food_identity.notes: Canonical English name 'Grilled Chicken Cartilage Skewer' is precise and unambiguous. Chinese (simplified and traditional), Malay, Tamil, and Hindi local names are all present, satisfying the Singaporean dish multilingual requirement.

q3_secondary_nutrient_completeness.notes: dietary_fibre_g (0.33), sodium_mg (600.0), saturated_fat_g (5.0), and sugar_g (1.67) are all present as numeric values and are plausible for a soy-sauce-marinated grilled chicken cartilage product.

q4_glycemic_completeness.notes: glycemic_index is set to numeric 0 (required to be numeric per scoring model; sentinel value indicating not-applicable for negligible-carbohydrate food), gi_category is 'not_applicable', glycemic_load_per_serving is 0.0, and gi_source is documented with full rationale referencing University of Sydney GI Database. This satisfies the numeric requirement and the low/zero-carbohydrate GI rule.
